publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.p: German Evangelical Church vicar in the Rhineland, friend of Reinhardt Oppel.

div.head: Career Summary
div.p: Walter Albert Engelhardt was born in Hannover. After war service and a period of study, in 1925 he moved to the Rhineland. From 1929 to 1940, he served as the vicar (Pfarrer) of Leisel (village about 80km (50 miles) west of Frankfurt-am-Main), then Essen-Altenessen 1947–49, then as a hospital chaplain 1951–59. Engelhardt published extensively on music and liturgical history. He was also involved in reform of the Evangelical Church Hymnal. His papers are preserved in the Archive of the Evangelical Church in the Rhineland, Düsseldorf.

div.head: Engelhardt and Reinhardt Oppel
div.p: Reinhardt Oppel once referred to Leisel as “a small Hunsrück eyrie” (Hunsrücknest) – the area around Hunsrück being what Oppel called his “ancestral land,” to which he regularly returned in vacations. Like Engelhardt, Oppel was a protestant Christian; in his correspondence with Schenker in 1933 and 1934 Oppel referred to Engelhardt as a “theologian” as well as a “friend.” Oppel reports that Engelhardt was planning to edit a new edition of Albert Schweitzer’s book J. S. Bach: Le Musicien-Poète (Leipzig: Breitkopf & Härtel, 1905), which Schweitzer had rewritten in two volumes in German in 1908. Oppel advised Engelhardt against doing so on the grounds that Schweitzer was “not sufficiently musical to deal thoroughly with Bach,” and the project was thus “entirely futile” (OJ 13/10, [164]). Oppel gave Engelhardt instruction in Schenker’s theory, and when it was published Engelhardt was “very impressed by” Oswald Jonas’s book Das Wesen des musikalischen Kunstwerks (1934).

div.head: Engelhardt and Schenker
div.p: No correspondence between Engelhardt and Heinrich Schenker is known to have taken place, but on at least one occasion Oppel forwarded a letter that he had received from Engelhardt. In his diary, Schenker called him a “music theologian.” Two letters from Engelhardt to Jeanette Schenker are preserved as OJ 10/21, [1] and [2], from 1938 and 1939.

ref.title: Archiv der Evangelischen Kirche im Rheinland: 7NL 101
